channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Rubio: U.S.-Iran War Could Last Another 2 to 4 Weeks
item.description: The White House remains committed to achieving all of its war objectives despite a lack of NATO support.

item.title: The nightmare scenario for global trade
item.description: There are worse blockages than the Strait of Hormuz, our analysis finds

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: Advantage Iran
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: After the Nation-State
item.description: A slew of new doomsaying books miss what’s coming round the bend.

item.title: The United States of Westeros
item.description: “A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms,” like its predecessor series, offers a prescient glimpse into contemporary politics.

item.title: The Iran War’s Economic Winners and Losers
item.description: Is the Persian Gulf’s economic model in jeopardy?

item.title: The Birds That Run the Land
item.description: Indigenous knowledge is helping conservationists manage delicate ecologies.

item.title: Should you track your VO2 max?
item.description: Treat it is a measure, not a target

item.title: Ukraine and Russia Are Warring at Tennis
item.description: Festering battles on the international tennis circuit have finally burst into the open.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of March 21: Denmark and Italy vote, an Afghan-Pakistani cease-fire ends, and Argentines march in Buenos Aires.

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The war that shaped modern Iran
item.description: Iran’s conflict with Iraq in the 1980s provides valuable lessons for today, writes Richard Cockett, a senior editor

item.title: The Only Boots on the Ground in Iran Should Be IAEA Inspectors
item.description: The only appropriate authority that can account for and monitor Iran’s nuclear stockpile is the International Atomic Energy Agency, which is legally mandated to do so under the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons. No one else has the expertise or the independence to provide oversight without further escalating the conflict.

item.title: The Iran War: Is Southeast Asia Heading Into Its Next Crisis?
item.description: History suggests the energy crunch is just the beginning of a chain reaction that ends with a food and social stability crisis.

item.title: Australia, International Broadcasting, and the Maintenance of Regional Trust
item.description: Australia must decide whether to remain an active, trusted participant in regional discourse or to relinquish that role to competing actors.

item.title: Has the Iran War Revealed a Shift in India’s Grand Strategy?
item.description: Whatever happened to India’s promotion of a multipolar world?

item.title: Turkey’s Contested Homefront
item.description: What lesson about national solidarity will Erdogan draw from Israel and Iran?

item.title: Cracks Spread Through Putin’s Power Structure
item.description: War setbacks, protests, and internet shutdowns expose the limits of Kremlin control.

item.title: What the West Misses About China’s Nuclear Build-up
item.description: Until Western analysts honestly grapple with what U.S. and allied conventional capabilities look like from Beijing, no stable strategic relationship with China is achievable.

item.title: Can America and Iran Reach a Cease-Fire?
item.description: Both sides want to dictate the terms—but neither truly can.

item.title: The Global Implications of China’s 5-Year Plan AI Ambitions
item.description: China’s quest for global leadership in AI and cybersecurity threatens free expression and information integrity around the world.

item.title: The Global Economy’s Many Chokepoints
item.description: In a highly decentralized and competitive network, investors are incentivized to optimize for efficiency rather than for resilience. This dynamic has produced a highly fragile global economy, owing to single points of failure that extend far beyond geographical bottlenecks like the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Despite a Supposedly Defensive Policy, China’s Military Budget Rises Fast
item.description: China’s sustained higher defense spending has altered the military balance in the region.

item.title: Australia, New Zealand Set Ambitious Course for Deeper Defense Cooperation
item.description: Building on the long-standing bilateral alliance under the ANZUS Treaty, Canberra and Wellington aim to translate political commitments into greater practical military cooperation by 2035.

item.title: Europe’s China Confusion: From Global Trade to Russia
item.description: From trade issues to the Russia-Ukraine war, China presents a dual face to Europe.

item.title: Latin American Countries Boost Ties to Africa
item.description: Can this era of south-south cooperation outlast leftist leadership?

item.title: The Rise of the Chinese Platform State
item.description: The West has long viewed China’s innovation system as purely top-down, but the reality is more complicated. The government functions less like a central planner and more like a platform company, structuring markets so that firms compete intensely and strengthen national capabilities.

item.title: Kyrgyzstan Is Slouching Back Toward Illiberalism
item.description: President Sadyr Japarov has stifled opposition. Now he’s going after his former right-hand man.

item.title: Interdependence Bites Back
item.description: In today’s interconnected global economy, geopolitical shocks cascade through trade, production, and financial networks that were built for efficiency, not resilience. As disruptions hit critical supply chains, temporary price spikes can evolve into sustained inflationary pressures, raising the risk of stagflation.

item.title: How an Indonesian Pulp Giant Built a Carbon Project to Save a Forest From Itself
item.description: A months-long investigation into APRIL Group’s flagship Riau Ecosytem Restoration reveals how its carbon project was built on a hypothetical deforestation threat that is unlikely to materialize.

item.title: Takaichi Sanae and the Constitutional Revision Debate
item.description: It’s not as simple as China would have it.

item.title: What Does North Korea Think of Japan’s Prime Minister?
item.description: The country’s newspaper of record has been noticeably silent of late.

item.title: America’s New Way of Economic War
item.description: The dangers of a strategy with no doctrine.

item.title: The War in Iran Could Become Like the War in Ukraine
item.description: How America can avoid a Russian-style quagmire.

item.title: The Price of Strategic Incoherence in Iran
item.description: For America, the war’s benefits won’t outweigh its costs.

item.title: Shopee Finally Became Profitable in 2025
item.description: Southeast Asia's big consumer-facing tech firms are finally starting to fulfill investors' expectations.

item.title: The Strait of Hormuz Is Burning, But China Is Not Panicking
item.description: China appears better insulated than most against the energy shocks emerging from the latest Middle East conflict.

item.title: TPP Founder Ko Wen-je Gets 17 Years in Prison
item.description: Ko’s corruption scandal has roiled the third largest party in Taiwan’s legislature.

item.title: Trump Must Act Quickly to Protect Diego Garcia
item.description: The Iran war once again proved the base’s importance. To ensure that the U.S. military keeps access for decades to come, Trump must endorse the Mauritius-U.K. Chagos agreement.
